data.title: nLIGHT Laser Sensing Contracts Shape New Growth Engine And Risks
data.description:
• nLIGHT's laser sensing business has secured a $50 million contract tied to an established missile program.
  • The company has started production for a new classified sensing project while existing sensing programs are running at full capacity.
  • Laser sensing is emerging as a core revenue driver as nLIGHT expands beyond its traditional focus on defense lasers.

For investors tracking NasdaqGS:LASR, the new laser sensing wins add an important layer to the story. The stock trades at $65.37 and has seen very large gains over the past year, with a 70.4% return year to date and a 12.3% return over the past week. In that context, this contract activity highlights how nLIGHT is building out a second pillar alongside its better known defense laser business.

The launch of production on a classified sensing program, combined with the $50 million missile related award, indicates that laser sensing could become a more meaningful part of the business mix. Readers may want to watch how management discusses this segment over coming quarters, including any commentary on program timing, capacity additions and the balance between defense and commercial sensing applications.

Stay updated on the most important news stories for nLIGHT by adding it to your watchlist or portfolio. Alternatively, explore our Community to discover new perspectives on nLIGHT.

📰 Beyond the headline: 2 risks and 1 thing going right for nLIGHT that every investor should see.

For nLIGHT, the sensing wins look like a logical extension of the directed-energy story rather than a side project. The $50 million missile contract is tied to a long-running program, which can help with visibility, while the classified build and fully loaded existing lines point to tight capacity. That is encouraging if you see nLIGHT as a supplier of mission-critical optics and lasers, but it also increases execution risk, because any slip in qualifying hardware, yields or delivery schedules could affect multiple programs at once. As the company expands beyond pure defense lasers into sensing, the competitive set also broadens to include players such as L3Harris, Teledyne and Leonardo DRS, so investors may want to watch whether nLIGHT can keep its technical edge and pricing power as sensing scales.

Advertisement

How This Fits Into The nLIGHT Narrative

  • The narrative highlights expansion in higher margin verticals such as laser sensing. These new contracts directly support that by adding funded programs and production volume to the sensing pipeline.
  • At the same time, tying sensing to a missile platform still concentrates exposure in defense budgets. This runs against the narrative goal of reducing reliance on a small group of programs and customers.
  • The narrative focuses heavily on high energy weapon systems, so the contract mix, timing and margins specific to sensing projects may not be fully captured in existing storylines investors are using.

Knowing what a company is worth starts with understanding its story. Check out one of the top narratives in the Simply Wall St Community for nLIGHT to help decide what it is worth to you.

The Risks and Rewards Investors Should Consider

  • ⚠️ Higher dependence on U.S. defense and missile programs, where changes in funding, priorities or contract timing could affect both directed energy and sensing revenue at the same time.
  • ⚠️ Sensing lines already running at full capacity leave limited room for operational missteps, so any ramp, quality or supply chain issue could pressure margins and delivery schedules.
  • 🎁 A growing laser sensing business provides a second revenue engine alongside defense lasers, which can help broaden nLIGHT’s product mix and strengthen its role on complex platforms.
  • 🎁 Analysts have flagged strong earnings growth potential, and incremental, contract-backed sensing work can add to that earnings runway if execution stays on track.

What To Watch Going Forward

From here, consider how quickly sensing revenue shows up in reported segment numbers, and whether management starts breaking out more detail on this line of business. Contract follow-ons, new platform wins and any commentary on adding manufacturing capacity can help indicate how durable this new engine might be. It is also worth tracking how sensing exposure is balanced between missile, other defense and potential commercial uses, since that mix will shape both risk and margin quality over time.

data.title: Is It Time To Reassess Duolingo (DUOL) After A 49% Year To Date Share Price Slide
data.description:
• Wondering whether Duolingo's current share price reflects its true worth, or if the recent weakness is setting up a more interesting entry point.
  • The stock closed at US$90.03, with a 6.7% decline over the past week, an 8.5% decline over the past month, and a 49.0% decline year to date, while the 1-year return sits at a 72.7% decline and the 3-year return at a 33.3% decline.
  • Recent coverage around Duolingo continues to focus on its place in online education and the scale of its user base. This keeps the company on many investors' watchlists. This context matters because sentiment around the broader digital learning space often feeds directly into how quickly investors reassess risk and potential for the shares.
  • On Simply Wall St's 6-point valuation checklist, Duolingo currently scores 4 out of 6. Next up is a closer look at what different valuation methods say about the stock and why a more narrative-driven approach to valuation at the end of this article might give you an even clearer view.

Find out why Duolingo's -72.7% return over the last year is lagging behind its peers.

Advertisement

Approach 1: Duolingo Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow, or DCF, model projects a company’s future cash flows and then discounts them back to today’s dollars, aiming to estimate what the entire business could be worth right now.

For Duolingo, the model used is a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity approach based on cash flow projections. The latest twelve month Free Cash Flow is about $360.0 million. Analysts provide specific estimates for the next few years, and Simply Wall St extrapolates further out. This includes a 2035 Free Cash Flow projection of $538.3 million in this dataset, with each future figure discounted back to today using a required rate of return.

Putting all those discounted cash flows together, the DCF model arrives at an estimated intrinsic value of about $236.16 per share. Compared with the recent share price of US$90.03, this implies the stock is roughly 61.9% below that DCF estimate. On this model alone, this suggests the shares screen as materially undervalued.

Result: UNDERVALUED

Our Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) analysis suggests Duolingo is undervalued by 61.9%. Track this in your watchlist or portfolio, or discover 58 more high quality undervalued stocks.

Head to the Valuation section of our Company Report for more details on how we arrive at this Fair Value for Duolingo.

Approach 2: Duolingo Price vs Earnings

For a profitable company, the P/E ratio is a useful way to think about value because it connects what you pay for each share with the earnings that the business is currently generating. Higher growth expectations or lower perceived risk often justify a higher P/E, while slower growth or higher risk usually points to a lower, more conservative P/E being reasonable.

Duolingo currently trades on a P/E of 10.21x. This sits below both the Consumer Services industry average P/E of 17.81x and the peer group average of 18.52x. Simply Wall St also calculates a proprietary “Fair Ratio” of 6.07x for Duolingo. This is the P/E level that would be expected given factors such as the company’s earnings growth profile, its industry, profit margins, market cap and key risks.

The Fair Ratio is more tailored than a simple comparison with peers or the broad industry because it adjusts for Duolingo’s specific characteristics rather than assuming all companies should trade on the same multiple. Setting the current P/E of 10.21x against the Fair Ratio of 6.07x indicates that the shares are pricing in a richer earnings multiple than that model supports.

Result: OVERVALUED

P/E ratios tell one story, but what if the real opportunity lies elsewhere? Start investing in legacies, not executives. Discover our 18 top founder-led companies.

Upgrade Your Decision Making: Choose your Duolingo Narrative

Earlier it was mentioned that there is an even better way to understand valuation, so meet Narratives, a simple way for you to attach a clear story about Duolingo to the numbers you see on screen, including your own view of fair value and assumptions for future revenue, earnings and margins.

A Narrative on Simply Wall St links three pieces together: your view of Duolingo’s business story, a financial forecast that reflects that story, and a resulting fair value that you can compare to the current share price to decide whether the stock looks expensive or cheap to you.

These Narratives sit on the Community page and are easy to use. You can see how millions of other investors are thinking, then adjust the inputs to match your own expectations rather than relying on a single static valuation model.

They also update automatically when fresh information comes in. If Duolingo reports new earnings or news about Vision 2026, the Narrative’s forecast and fair value move with it instead of staying frozen at last quarter’s assumptions.

For example, one Duolingo Narrative on Simply Wall St currently sets fair value at about US$105.73 while another, more optimistic one, points to around US$352.01. Approach 1: Phibro Animal Health Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow, or DCF, model looks at the cash Phibro Animal Health is expected to generate in the future and then discounts those cash flows back to today to estimate what the business may be worth now.

Phibro Animal Health most recently reported Free Cash Flow of about $42.3 million. Using a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity model, analysts and extrapolated estimates point to Free Cash Flow reaching $246.9 million in 2035, with interim years such as 2026 and 2028 projected at $95.7 million and $135.7 million respectively. All of these cash flows are in US$ and are discounted back using the DCF framework set out by Simply Wall St.

On this basis, the model estimates an intrinsic value of roughly $119.16 per share. Compared with the latest share price of $57.89, this implies the stock is around 51.4% undervalued using these inputs and assumptions.

Approach 2: Phibro Animal Health Price vs Earnings

For a profitable business, the P/E ratio is a simple way to see what investors are currently paying for each dollar of earnings. It connects directly to the bottom line, which is usually the main driver of long term shareholder returns.

In general, higher growth expectations and lower perceived risk can justify a higher P/E ratio, while slower growth or higher risk tend to support a lower, more conservative P/E. So the question is what looks like a reasonable or "normal" P/E for Phibro Animal Health today.

Phibro Animal Health currently trades on a P/E of 25.48x. That sits above the Pharmaceuticals industry average of 16.97x and the broader peer average of 11.59x. data.title: Eli Lilly Balances India GLP-1 Generics Shock With Foundayo U.S. Push
data.description:
• Eli Lilly (NYSE:LLY) is seeing rapid market share declines in India for its GLP-1 obesity drugs as generic competitors expand following patent expirations.
  • At the same time, Lilly is rolling out its new oral obesity pill Foundayo in the U.S. through online pharmacies, retail chains, and telehealth platforms.
  • Foundayo is now available via Amazon Pharmacy, GoodRx, Ro, LifeMD, and major retail pharmacies, with both self pay and insured options for American patients.
  • The combination of pressure from generics in India and wider access channels in the U.S. raises fresh questions about the long term shape of Lilly’s obesity business.

Eli Lilly, trading at $939.47 per share, sits at the center of a fast changing obesity treatment market. The stock is up 29.2% over the past year and has returned 156.3% over three years, while the year to date move shows a 13.0% decline. Against that backdrop, investors now have to weigh fresh competition in India against a broad U.S. launch of Foundayo.

For you as an investor, the key question is how much the U.S. direct to consumer expansion can offset or complement pressure from Indian generics over time. This mix of weaker pricing power abroad and wider access at home could reshape revenue sources within Lilly’s obesity franchise and may influence how markets think about the risk and reward profile of NYSE:LLY.

Stay updated on the most important news stories for Eli Lilly by adding it to your watchlist or portfolio. Alternatively, explore our Community to discover new perspectives on Eli Lilly.

See which insiders are buying and buying and selling Eli Lilly following this latest news.

The India and U.S. developments point in different directions for Eli Lilly’s GLP-1 franchise, which matters if you are thinking about how durable current expectations are. In India, cheaper semaglutide generics are already chipping away at Lilly’s market share in weight loss treatments, which shows how quickly pricing power can weaken once patents fall away and local manufacturers move in. At the same time, Foundayo’s U.S. rollout uses almost every high visibility channel available, from Amazon Pharmacy’s same day delivery and One Medical kiosks to GoodRx discounts, Ro’s telehealth offering and LillyDirect. That structure keeps Lilly closely connected to the prescription and payment flows even when patients pay cash, which may appeal to investors who care about volume and data visibility as well as price.

Advertisement

How This Fits Into The Eli Lilly Narrative

  • The broad Foundayo launch through Amazon, GoodRx, Ro, LifeMD and LillyDirect aligns with the community narrative that highlights obesity and diabetes drugs plus digital access models as key growth drivers.
  • India’s fast growing GLP-1 generics market challenges the assumption that high-price GLP-1 treatments will retain strong pricing power everywhere, especially once patents expire or local competitors arrive.
  • The narrative focuses heavily on global expansion and capacity build out, while this India specific market share pressure may not be fully reflected in long term assumptions about international contribution and margins.

Knowing what a company is worth starts with understanding its story. Check out one of the top narratives in the Simply Wall St Community for Eli Lilly to help decide what it's worth to you.

The Risks and Rewards Investors Should Consider

  • ⚠️ Investors face pricing and competition risk if India’s experience with GLP-1 generics spreads to other markets, putting pressure on Lilly’s premium obesity drugs over time.
  • ⚠️ Analysts highlight two key risks, including a high level of non cash earnings and balance sheet leverage, so it may be useful to see how new GLP-1 investments and partnerships flow through reported profits and debt.
  • 🎁 Foundayo’s presence on Amazon Pharmacy, GoodRx, Ro and other platforms offers broad patient access with clear self pay pricing, which can support prescription volumes even where insurance coverage for obesity drugs is patchy.
  • 🎁 The wider GLP-1 portfolio in obesity and diabetes gives Lilly multiple touchpoints with patients and prescribers, which could help offset regional market share shifts like those currently seen in India.

What To Watch Going Forward

From here, focus on how U.S. Foundayo uptake trends across Amazon Pharmacy, GoodRx, Ro, LifeMD and retail channels, and whether Lilly or third parties provide any color on prescription volumes or refill rates. In parallel, the India GLP-1 segment is a live test of how quickly generics can compress market share and pricing for branded therapies, so further shifts there are worth tracking if you are thinking about long term GLP-1 cash flows. It may also help to watch future commentary from competitors such as Novo Nordisk and any updates on reimbursement policies for obesity drugs in large markets, as these factors can reshape the balance between price and volume for Lilly’s obesity business.
